title: Dog Swallowed a Chicken Bone: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Immediate steps and emergency advice if your dog swallowed a chicken bone. Learn why this is high-urgency and how a photo helps your veterinarian.
content_html:
TL;DR: Swallowing chicken bones is a high-urgency medical situation because they can splinter and cause life-threatening internal punctures. Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately if your dog shows signs of distress, vomiting, or pain.

What are the risks if my dog swallows a chicken bone?

When a dog swallows a chicken bone, it poses several serious health risks:

  • Unlike recreational chew bones, chicken bones—especially cooked ones—are hollow and brittle.
  • Bones can easily splinter into sharp shards as they pass through the digestive system.
  • Splinters can cause punctures or tears in the esophagus, stomach, or intestines.

How urgent is it if my dog eats a chicken bone?

Ingesting a chicken bone is a high-urgency situation. To ensure your pet's safety, you should:

  • Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately to discuss the risk of gastrointestinal perforation or bowel obstruction.
  • Monitor your dog closely for signs of gagging or repetitive vomiting.
  • Watch for symptoms of intense pain or a bloated abdomen.

Why should I take a photo of the chicken bone my dog ate?

Visual information is critical in determining the necessary medical intervention. If you have access to the rest of the meal or a similar bone, please do the following:

  • Take a clear photograph to help the triage team assess the size, sharpness, and type of bone involved.
  • Provide this evidence to help the veterinarian decide if inducing vomiting is safe or if immediate surgery is required.
  • Wait for professional guidance, as inducing vomiting with sharp objects can cause further internal damage.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Esophageal foreign bodies are more common in dogs than cats, with bones being a frequent cause of obstruction. Common locations for lodging include the thoracic inlet, over the heart base, or the caudal esophagus cranial to the diaphragm. Clinical signs include ptyalism, gagging, dysphagia, regurgitation, and repeated attempts to swallow. Passage of bone material in the stomach is usually resolved by normal digestive processes. If a bone lodges in the esophagus, endoscopic retrieval or surgical removal via gastrotomy may be necessary, especially if a perforation has occurred or endoscopy is unsuccessful. If the animal is clinically stable, passage of bone fragments can be monitored with serial radiographs. Surgical intervention is indicated if the object fails to pass within 48 hours, serial radiographs show no movement, or clinical signs deteriorate. Esophagitis, if present, should be treated appropriately.

Chapter: Gastroenterology, Surgery, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 391)

faqs.question: Should I try to make my dog vomit at home?
faqs.answer: No. Never induce vomiting unless specifically instructed by a veterinarian. If the bone is sharp, it can cause more damage to the throat or esophagus coming back up than it would staying in the stomach.

faqs.question: How long does it take for a bone to pass?
faqs.answer: It typically takes 24 to 48 hours for objects to pass through a dog's system. However, if the bone is stuck, it will not pass on its own and requires medical intervention.

faqs.question: What are the warning signs of internal injury?
faqs.answer: Watch for lethargy, loss of appetite, straining to defecate, bloody stools, or a painful, hunched-over posture. These are signs of a potential emergency.
